channel.title: Posts on allokkio
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on allokkio

item.title: A little Animation-Test
item.description:
My first try at p5js. A little animation that plots a circle, with a radius determined by Guassian noise.

Code:

// variable definitions:
var c_size = 450
var time = 0.0;
var increment = 0.001;
var time_to_update = time + 2*increment;
var increment_to_update = 0.01;
var n = 0

// canvas setup
function setup() {
  background("#DDDCD3");
  var canvas = createCanvas(c_size, c_size);
  canvas.parent('sketch-holder');
}

// generation and drawing
function draw() {
  noStroke();
  time += increment;
  if (time >= time_to_update) {
    n = abs(randomGaussian(1,c_pad/2));
    time_to_update += increment_to_update

    rect_color = color(221,220,211,50);
    fill(rect_color, 2);
    rect(0, 0, c_size, c_size);
    fill("#1e90ff");
    ellipse(c_size/2, c_size/2, n, n);
  }
    stroke("#ffa500")
    strokeWeight(1)
    fill(221,220,211,0);
    ellipse(c_size/2, c_size/2, c_pad/2, c_pad/2);
    ellipse(c_size/2, c_size/2 ,c_pad, c_pad);
    // text
    textSize(19);
    noStroke( );
    fill("#ffa500");
    text("~68% of \n  samples", c_pad/4 + 18, c_size/2);
    text("~95% of \n  samples", 18, c_size/2);
}
}

item.title: First Steps with Tydy Evaluation
item.description:
‘Multiple exclamation marks,’ he went on, shaking his head, ‘are a sure sign of a diseased mind.’ - Eric, Terry Pratchett

The purpose of this post is to start a protocol my process for getting used to tidy evaluation. My actual course of action was a bit more unstructured than the one presented here. But not by much I wanted to preserve at least some degree of sanity for eventual re-reads. My hope is that it will help me to evaluate my current learning practice (Sorry, I did not find any better word), and maybe also help some lost souls to build up their intuition too.

Tidy evaluation is a form of non standard evaluation (NSE) recently put forward by the mighty Hadley Wickham. NSE is a very general term that basically refers to everything which does not follow the usual R conventions of evaluation. Nowadays, R users are already
familiar with NSE, through formulas and dplyr. Now, tidy evaluation provides an entire toolkit to create domain-specific languages (DSL) of grammars.

When I try to get used to a concept I value intuition and conceptual understanding more than complete understanding and (full) knowledge. I therefore like to start by testing out some functions with some pseudo data.

In this case I started with quo(), the quotation function. It captures the provided R code without evaluation (quotes), captures the current environment, and bundles them into what is called a quosure.

# load libraries:
library(rlang)
## Warning: Paket 'rlang' wurde unter R Version 3.4.2 erstellt
library(dplyr)
## Warning: Paket 'dplyr' wurde unter R Version 3.4.2 erstellt
## 
## Attache Paket: 'dplyr'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     filter, lag
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union
# quote test data and test equation:
my_data <- c(1,2,3,4,5)
my_data_quosure <- quo(my_data)
my_eq_quosure <- quo(1+2+3+4+5)

print(my_data_quosure)
## <quosure: global>
## ~my_data
print(my_eq_quosure)
## <quosure: global>
## ~1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5

As I expected the equation is not evaluated but transformed into a quosure which quotes my equation. What happened to my_data is interesting, because it is again not evaluated. I got to admit I was not thinking about that. Secondly, the print of quosure also provides us with information about the encapsulated environment of the quosure (which in both cases is the global environment).

The rlang package provides the function eval_tidy to evaluate quosures within their environment:

eval_tidy(my_data_quosure)
## [1] 1 2 3 4 5
eval_tidy(my_eq_quosure)
## [1] 15

How can the eval function from base R evaluate a quosure? By unquoting the quosure. Unquoting can be done with multiple operations. One of them is !! (read: bang-bang) operator (more info can be found in the well written help section of the rlang package, just type ?'!!' after the package is loaded). Here are some things I tested:

# check normal eval:
eval(1+2+3+4+5)
## [1] 15
eval("1+2+3+4+5")
## [1] "1+2+3+4+5"
eval(parse(text = "1+2+3+4+5"))
## [1] 15
# check eval with quosures:
eval(my_eq_quosure)
## <quosure: global>
## ~1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5
'!!'(my_eq_quosure)
## 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5
eval('!!'(my_eq_quosure))
## [1] 15
# test more options to use eval on quosures:
eval(UQE(my_eq_quosure))
## [1] 15
eval(get_expr(my_eq_quosure))
## [1] 15

That is pretty nice. In some way one can see quosures as an alternative to character strings and expressions.

Now, the !! operator is accompanied by the !!! operator (again, check the help). The bang-bang-bang operator (yep!) embeds unquotes the argument (like !!), but also splices the argument. Now, I could look up what splicing means exactly, but instead I just fiddle around with it and give some examples. First, I want to compare the behavior with regard to the existing quosures:

'!!'(my_data_quosure)
## my_data
'!!!'(my_data_quosure)
## [[1]]
## <quosure: global>
## ~my_data
#
'!!'(my_eq_quosure)
## 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5
'!!!'(my_eq_quosure)
## [[1]]
## <quosure: global>
## ~1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5

Ah, so !!! always enlists its arguments. That is good to know. Next, I wanted to get a feeling of how eval() would work with the !!!:

eval(my_eq_quosure)
## <quosure: global>
## ~1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5
eval('!!'(my_eq_quosure))
## [1] 15
eval('!!!'(my_eq_quosure)[[1]])
## <quosure: global>
## ~1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5
eval('!!'( '!!!'(my_eq_quosure)[[1]] ))
## [1] 15

The !!! operator becomes useful when multiple arguments are provided to a function, or when used to enquote multiple functions. Luckily, I found a nice example in the Should-tidyeval-be.abandoned section of the Rstudio community forum. I took the example and re-arranged, so that it fits the fiddling I’ve done so far. So, in the following quosures are used to create a set the functions (min, mean and max) within a list of quosures:

summary_funs <- c(min = quo(min(a)),
                 mean = quo(mean(a)),
                 max = quo(max(a))
                 )
class(summary_funs)
## [1] "list"

A look at summary_funs shows that it encapsulates three entries with quosure. These are

str(summary_funs)
## List of 3
##  $ min :<quosure: global>
## ~min(a)
##  $ mean:<quosure: global>
## ~mean(a)
##  $ max :<quosure: global>
## ~max(a)

Now, check out beautiful this works together with dplyr:

my_dataframe <- data.frame(a = my_data)
my_dataframe %>%
  summarize(!!!summary_funs)
##   min mean max
## 1   1    3   5

Ok, so far, so good. That is really all there is for my first session. Often I like to be lazy like that, to let ideas sink it. Yet, I hope to start a next session soon!!!

item.title: On Aggregation I
item.description:
## Warning: Paket 'tibble' wurde unter R Version 3.4.1 erstellt
## Warning: Paket 'dplyr' wurde unter R Version 3.4.2 erstellt

The Story Linear reservoirs form the basis building block of most conceptual rainfall-runoff models. They can be combined or extended in a non-linear fashion to account for many complex behaviors. Furthermore, on the grand scale of things they can be seen as approximations to the Saint-Venant equations and are thus even physically justified (somehow, also see: Klemes (1982) and Szilagyi and Szilösi-Nagy (2010)).

For now let us assume that we study a hydrological system that consists of multiple linear reservoirs. The response of each reservoir is defined by a single constant \(tab\) and the overall runoff of the system (which is also the only part that is measurable) is given by the sum of the individual runoffs.

We try to answer the following question by use of simulation:

Is it possible to use arithmetic mean of the reservoir constants to get a lumped reservoir which represents the behavior or response of the individual (“fine scale”) reservoirs

A Little bit of Hydrology

In their search for effective parameters hydrologist usually revert back to the concept of hydrological response units (HRUs). The idea is to search for regions which are spatially homogenous and lump them together. Afterwards the effective parameters are usually estimated by optimization.

A more modern approach, called multiscale-parameter regionalization (MPR), suggests to first estimate the parameters at the finest possible scale and aggregate the parameters themselves afterwards. To make the fine-scale estimation possible it is assumed that the model parameters can be explicitly defined by a functional relationship with landscape properties.

My PHD is devoted to these functional relationships, but in this document we shall focus on the properties of the aggregation.

The Setup

Let us start with the experimental setup by defining a functional that returns a parameterized linear reservoir as a function of \(tab\).

spawn_linres <- function(tab) {
    q0 <- 0
    s <- 0
    linres <- function(qin) {
      qin - (tab*qin-s)*(1.-exp(-1/tab))
      q_out <- qin - (tab*qin-s)*(1.-exp(-1/tab))
      s <<- s + qin -q_out
      return(q_out)
    }
    return(linres)
}

The following figure uses the functional to exemplify how different reservoirs behave with respect to different values of \(tab\) (in relation to a unit input of 20)

We can see that small values of \(tab\) lead to responsive behavior with fast depletion (orange lines), while large values of \(tab\) lead to a dampened response with long memory (slow depletion, blue lines). Ok, now lets do the analysis.

The Simple Case: From 2 to 1

First, let us say that we have two linear reservoirs \(n=2\) which ought to be represented by a single one. To keep things simple let us also assume that both have the same input. We can then generate a sample of different \(tab\) values and check how good the different means of the \(tab\) represent the overall behavior of the system. We will use the venerable Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency, short \(NSE\) (i.e. the \(R^2\) of the runoff) as a distance measure/objective function.

Symmetry

Let us start by plotting an exemplary hydrograph from the generated samples. The overall (i.e. real) response of the system is depicted in blue, the individual fine-scale reservoirs in violet-tones and the response from the aggregated/averaged reservoir is shown in orange:

Ok! Now we can make a plot which shows the \(NSE\) values of the aggregated reservoir in dependence of the sampled “fine-scaled” \(tab_i\) values. We can see that the best (highest) \(NSE\) values are on the 1:1 line. This makes sense, because there the relation \(tab_1 = tab_2\)holds (and the inputs are the same). One can also see that the behavior is symmetric with regard to the line. This is also an effect of the equal inputs, since \(tab_1 + tab_2 \equiv tab_2 + tab_1\). We can also observe that the aggregation works better for larger \(tab\) values. This is mainly because of the behavior of the linear reservoirs (see: first section): More dampened functions exhibit smaller errors. Thus, a better \(NSE\) value can be expected.

The behavior and sensitivity of the results depends on the basin properties and on the chosen objective function. An example is given in the following figure, which shows that the sum of absolute relative errors (\(e_rel\)) is less forgiving than the \(NSE\):

The Asymmetric Case

How do the result change if the input for the reservoirs is different? Not much! The 1:1 line does not make a symmetry anymore, but higher \(tab\) values are still easier to aggregate (whatever the means). Here is an example which uses the same input as above for one of the fine scaled reservoirs, but a different input for the other one.

We can note two things. (a) With regard to the \(NSE\): the \(tab_2\) values are now more insensitive, while the \(tab_1\) values still exhibit the same pattern. And (b) The sensitivity pattern of \(e_rel\) is however inverted! The aggregation is more sensitive to changes of \(tab_2\), than to changes of \(tab_1\).

Summary

In summary we can say that perfect aggregation with the arithmetic mean is possible in the trivial case, where \(tab_1 = tab_2\) (independent of the input). For all other cases the mean is an imperfect approximation. In general, the quality of the approximations is better (less sensitive) for larger \(tab\) values. The quality decline (of the approximation) is dependent on basin properties (in our case the input) and the chosen objective functions. Averaging seems to be more difficult if one is interested in relative measures.

Higher Dimensions: From 3 to 1

Do these findings hold true in higher dimensions? To answer that question, let us try to aggregate 3 fine-scaled reservoirs next and see what happens.

To do so we will (again) use different inputs, as shown here:

Glimpses of the Cube

The result now has for dimensions, but We can still think of it as a cube: The different \(tab_i\) form the axis and the information of the objective function can be defined with the help of colors.

Here are some views of the cubes for the \(NSE\) and the \(e_{rel}\) distance measures. Some information gets lost because of the static 3d views. We therefore added the plotly code to create them, in case you want to explore the pattern yourself.

\(NSE\) cube
\(e_{rel}\) cube

all_cubes_erel

# nse Cube:
plotly::plot_ly(tab3_results,
        x = ~tab_1,
        y = ~tab_2,
        z = ~tab_3,
        color = ~nse_m,
        size = ~le_size,
        sizes = c(0,20),
        colors   = c("darkcyan","lightgreen","lightyellow"))
# rel_error cube:
plotly::plot_ly(tab3_results,
        x = ~tab_1,
        y = ~tab_2,
        z = ~tab_3,
        color = ~rel_error,
        size = ~le_size,
        sizes = c(0,20),
        colors   = c("darkcyan","lightgreen","lightyellow"))

Slice & Dice

The cubes from above give an impression of the overall patter of the objective functions. Now, we will look at them in detail by inspecting different subviews of both cubes:

Average Dices

slice_average

The following plots show the average view for each pixel or a given side. As depicted above one can think of it as looking from one side (marked in green) at the cube and seeing the average samples along the view-axis.

Horizontal Slices

horizontal_slice

The horizontal slices view a plain of the cube by fixing the \(tab_{3}\) at one value for each slice. As shown in the figure above, we made three slices, one for low-, one for mid- and one for high-values of \(tab_{3}\). In the following we thus have six figures in total (three for the \(NSE\) and three for the \(e_{rel}\)).

Summary

In this section we showed the relations between the behavior of three single reservoirs and the behavior that results from aggregating their respective constants \(tab_i\). We saw that the trivial case, i.e. the 1:1:1 line (where \(tab_1 = tab_2 = tab_3\)), results in perfect aggregation (for both, the \(NSE\) and the \(e_rel\)). It can also be seen that higher \(tab\) values are associated with better objective functions values (in the averaged views this can be especially well seen).

Is there a Solution?

Lastly, let us consider the following question:

Can a single reservoirs is able to represent multiple reservoirs at all?

For this we will optimize the previously aggregated \(tab\) value of the single reservoir for each sampled point. We will do this for the asymmetric case of two \(tab\) values and the asymmetric case of three \(tab\) values.

For each sample point we will use a simple derivative free optimization routine provided by stats::optimize (it actually is a FORTRAN code, which combines the golden section search and successive parabolic interpolation).

Asymmetric case with two \(tab\) values

In the following are the results for the asymmetric case of two \(tab\) values represented by a single reservoir. Note, the asymmetry of values for the \(NSE\) and decay away from the 1:1 line.

Asymmetric Case with three \(tab\) values

For the 3-dimensional case we will show the results in the form of the horizontal slices (cross sections) that have been presented above. (the respective code is provided below).

Comparison

We can compare the first exercise with the second one by comparing their densities and see that the 2 dimensional case has more extremely results, but for the \(NSE\) also larger deviations towards bad values. The 3 dimensional case has less perfect values, but also smaller deviations towards the worse performance

##             used   (Mb) gc trigger   (Mb)  max used   (Mb)
## Ncells   1176982   62.9    2164898  115.7   2164898  115.7
## Vcells 352219375 2687.3  555948719 4241.6 440669344 3362.1

Summary

In this section we see the same phenomenon as obtained by aggregation. Perfect approximations can be found along the 1:1 or the 1:1:1 line. Good Approximations are associated with larger \(tab\) values. The approximation potential for small \(tab\) is weak and strongly input dependent. Higher dimensional problems seem to have less probability to be optimizable, but also show dampened behavior if the results are not considered in a relative fashion.

Appendix: Sketches & Co

A small test to see if the optimized “aggregation”-parameters are within the bounds of the fine scaled parameters. Here a sketch for 3 dimension:

optimas_tab3_test <- optimas_tab3 %>%
  mutate(max_tab = pmax(tab_1,tab_2,tab_3),
         min_tab = pmin(tab_1,tab_2,tab_3),
         le_test = ifelse((tab_nse_opt >= min_tab & tab_nse_opt <= max_tab),1,0))


#
optimas_tab3_sub <- filter(optimas_tab3_test, tab_3 == g[20])
le_midpoint <- with(optimas_tab3_test, {mean( c(max(nse_opt),min(nse_opt)) )})
ggplot(optimas_tab3_sub) +
  geom_raster(aes(tab_1, tab_2, fill = le_test)) +
  geom_abline(color = "black") +
  labs(title = "Relative Error",
       subtitle = "Transect for 'low' tab3 Values") +
  coord_fixed( ) +
  theme_light( ) +
  theme(plot.background = element_rect(fill = '#EEEDE4'), 
        legend.background = element_rect(fill = '#EEEDE4'))

References

Klemes, Vit. 1982. “The Essence of Mathemtical Models of Reservoir Storage.” Canadian Journal of Civil Engineering. doi:10.1139/l82-072.

Szilagyi, Josef, and Andras Szilösi-Nagy. 2010. “Recursive Streamflow Forecasting: A State-Space Approach.”

item.title: Rainy Meanderings
item.description:
Here I will collect notes and references about the conceptualization in hydrology.

Klemes (Conceptualization and scale in hydrology, 1986; https://doi.org/10.1016/0022-1694(83)90208-1) provides the basics. The abstract reads:

It is argued that the levels of scale at which a meaningful conceptualization of physical processes is possible are not arbitrary and their range is not continuous. Formulations appropriate at a given level usually are not applicable at the immediately adjoining levels. This is seen as one of the important reasons for the slow progress of hydrological science on basin scale since it is dominated by different effects than those observed at the immediately lower level of channel reaches and hillslopes which represent the focus of current hydrological research. The search for an appropriate level of conceptualization can proceed either upward or downward along the hierarchy of scales. Both possibilities are discussed and an example of the downward route is given.

Linear and Non-Linear Reservoirs

Ostrowski provides the best derivation and description of linear and piece-wise linear reservoirs I am currently aware.

Handling Imperfection

Most conceptual rainfall-runoff models are biased estimators with relatively low variance. They are innately wrong, but are able to closely shadow the real system.

Again, a paper of Klemes is a good starting point. It seems that the paper Operational testing of hydrological simulation models (Klemes, 1986; http://dx.doi.org/10.1080/02626668609491024) introduced the calibration-validation concept to the hydrological community. Here is the abstract 1:

This paper describes a hierarchical scheme for the systematic testing of hydrological simulation models, a scheme that would lend to a model a minimum level of credibility commensurate with the difficulty of the modelling task. The scheme would be easy to implement operationally and its adoption would provide a convenient common basic standard applicable to models irrespective of their structure. The concept was motivated by problems encountered in connection with assessing the feasibility of using hydrological simulation models for the simulation of streamflow under changed climatic conditions. The problems arose while the author was working under the auspices of the World Meteorological Organization on a related project for the World Climate Programme - Water (Klemes, 1985b).

The underlying idea is by no means new and original since it is just commonsense tailored to specific hydrological situations. Essentially equivalent testing schemes are used in other sciences as a matter of standard routine. An outline of this testing scheme was originally presented in Klemes (1982a). Some elements of the concept appear in Lawson & Schiau (1977) and examples of application of the split-sample test, which represents the basic level of the scheme, can be found in WMO (1975, 1986).

Cross Validation will (most likely) not work on (highly) auto-correlated error-time-series. See the paper A note on the validity of cross-validation for evaluating autoregressive time series prediction (Christoph Bergmeir, Rob J Hyndman and Bonsoo Koo, 2017; to appear)). The abstract reads:

One of the most widely used standard procedures for model evaluation in classification and regression is \(K\)-fold cross-validation (CV). However, when it comes to time series forecasting, because of the inherent serial correlation and potential non-stationarity of the data, its application is not straightforward and often omitted by practitioners in favour of an out-of-sample (OOS) evaluation. In this paper, we show that in the case of a purely autoregressive model, the use of standard \(K\)-fold CV is possible as long as the models considered have uncorrelated errors. Such a setup occurs, for example, when the models nest a more appropriate model. This is very common when Machine Learning methods are used for prediction, where CV in particular is suitable to control for overfitting the data. We present theoretical insights supporting our arguments. Furthermore, we present a simulation study and a real-world example where we show empirically that \(K\)-fold CV performs favourably compared to both OOS evaluation and other time-series-specific techniques such as non-dependent cross-validation.

  1. I love the honesty of the abstract. It does not try to sell the thoughts as new or groundbreaking. I wonder if such a presentation is still possible today.↩

item.title: Visualizing Uncertainty
item.description:
I am not sure where one should start with this topic. Visualizing uncertainty is hard. in general, it is still an unsolved problem.

Back in the days even the mighty Nathan Yau wrote:

I still struggle with uncertainty and visualization. I haven’t seen many worthwhile solutions other than the old standbys, boxplots and histograms, which show distributions. But how many people understand spread, skew, etc? It’s a small proportion, which poses an interesting challenge.

Perhaps a good place to start the article When Lives Are on the Line by Robert Kosara. The article provides plenty of (good) examples and shows the inherent problems of uncertainty visualization. The underlying topic, i.e. Hurricane Forecasting, is also very relevant.

Real World Metaphors

This approach tries to visualize uncertainty by using visceral representation that are familiar to viewers. Example are brushstrokes (e.g. the thicker the more uncertain), hand-drawn style (e.g. the more sketchy the more uncertain) or watercolors (e.g. the more transparent the more uncertain). The used techniques might be summarized or at least strongly overlap with the domain of non-photorealistic rendering, which is

… an area of computer graphics that focuses on enabling a wide variety of expressive styles for digital art. In contrast to traditional computer graphics, which has focused on photorealism, NPR is inspired by artistic styles such as painting, drawing, technical illustration, and animated cartoons. NPR has appeared in movies and video games in the form of “toon shading”, as well as in scientific visualization, architectural illustration and experimental animation. An example of a modern use of this method is that of cel-shaded animation.

HANDY. In 2012 Wood et al. presented a framework for sketchy information visualization. The framework is called handy and is a processing library. Here is an excerpt from abstract:

We present and evaluate a framework for constructing sketchy style information visualizations that mimic data graphics drawn by hand. We provide an alternative renderer for the Processing graphics environment that redefines core drawing primitives including line, polygon and ellipse rendering. These primitives allow higher-level graphical features such as bar charts, line charts, treemaps and node-link diagrams to be drawn in a sketchy style with a specified degree of sketchiness. …

There also exists a great gallery, which exhibits many examples and a very thoughtful blogpost about the potential and use of sketchiness for visualizing uncertainty in general.

SEMIOTIC. Elijah Meeks is very smart- He created Semiotic.

Semiotic is (yet another) data visualization framework. It is based on react. The motivation behind it is to provide a fast way to create plots, without putting to much time into a specific chart.

It can visualize uncertainty by ‘sketchy’ rendering. Elijah wrote a very nice article which lays out his ideas.

Spaghetti

The idea here is to basically show a whole set of different scenarios congruently.

USING MOVEMENT. Instead of showing the whole ensembles at the same time one can reveal a “scenario” at a time. Of course this approach does not work on print. Here are some recent examples:

  • Global Warming

item.title: Intro
item.description:
Currently everything is very scrappy here and it will probably remain so for quite some time.

  • I will try to put some pretty random notes on random things on here.
  • The idea is that each “blogpost” will be expanded over time and is thus not finished. I may provide some kind of update news as the project goes along; that will however depend on how everything plays out.

Some Definitions and Concepts

scrap (/skɹæp/):

  1. A (small) piece; a fragment; a detached, incomplete portion.
  2. Discarded objects (especially metal) that may be dismantled to recover their constituent materials, junk.

uovo all’occhio di bue: Fried egg
